description: Main physical definitions that are not part of the main units.

words.name: second
words.description: Ambiguous word: either a unit to measure a duration or an ordinal position in a ranking (first, second, third, ...)

words.name: force
words.description: In physics, a force is an influence that can change the motion of an object; it has both magnitude and direction and is measured in newton.

words.name: mass
words.description: Mass is the quantity of matter in a body and a measure of its resistance to acceleration (inertia) when a force is applied; the SI unit is the kilogram.

words.name: energy
words.description: Energy is the quantitative property that is transferred to a body or system to perform work or to heat it; the SI unit is the joule.

words.name: power
words.description: Power is the amount of energy transferred or converted per unit time; the SI unit is the watt.

words.name: pressure
words.description: Pressure is the force applied perpendicular to the surface of an object per unit area; the SI unit is the pascal.

words.name: voltage
words.description: Voltage, also called electric potential difference, is the difference in electric potential between two points; the SI unit is the volt.

words.name: velocity
words.description: Velocity is the directional speed of a motion of an object as a function of time; it is the rate of change of an object's position with respect to a frame of reference.

words.name: acceleration
words.description: In mechanics, acceleration is the rate of change of the velocity of an object with respect to time.

words.name: displacement
words.description: In geometry and mechanics, a displacement is a vector whose length is the shortest distance from the initial to the final position of a point.

words.name: momentum
words.description: In Newtonian mechanics, momentum is the product of the mass and velocity of an object; it is a vector quantity.

words.name: impulse
words.description: In classical mechanics, impulse is the change in momentum of an object; it equals the integral of force over the time interval for which it acts.

words.name: work
words.description: In physics, work is the energy transferred to or from an object via the application of force along a displacement.

words.name: torque
words.description: In physics and mechanics, torque is the rotational equivalent of linear force; it is also referred to as the moment of force.

words.name: inertia
words.description: Inertia is the tendency of an object to maintain its state of motion unless acted upon by a force.

words.name: friction
words.description: Friction is the force resisting the relative motion of solid surfaces, fluid layers, and material elements sliding against each other.

words.name: gravity
words.description: In physics, gravity is a fundamental interaction which causes mutual attraction between all things that have mass.

words.name: density
words.description: The density of a substance is its mass per unit volume; the SI unit is the kilogram per cubic metre.

words.name: temperature
words.description: Temperature is a physical quantity that expresses quantitatively the perceptions of hotness and coldness.

words.name: heat
words.description: In thermodynamics, heat is the thermal energy transferred between systems due to a temperature difference.

words.name: entropy
words.description: Entropy is a scientific concept that is most commonly associated with a state of disorder, randomness, or uncertainty in a thermodynamic system.

words.name: enthalpy
words.description: Enthalpy is the sum of a thermodynamic system's internal energy and the product of its pressure and volume.

words.name: current
words.description: An electric current is a flow of charged particles, such as electrons or ions, moving through an electrical conductor or space.

words.name: resistance
words.description: The electrical resistance of an object is a measure of its opposition to the flow of electric current.

words.name: capacitance
words.description: Capacitance is the capability of a material object or device to store electric charge.

words.name: inductance
words.description: Inductance is the tendency of an electrical conductor to oppose a change in the electric current flowing through it.

words.name: permittivity
words.description: In electromagnetism, the permittivity is a measure of the electric polarizability of a dielectric.

words.name: permeability
words.description: In electromagnetism, permeability is the measure of magnetization that a material obtains in response to an applied magnetic field.

words.name: wavelength
words.description: In physics and mathematics, wavelength is the spatial period of a periodic wave, the distance over which the wave's shape repeats.

words.name: wave
words.description: In physics, a wave is a propagating dynamic disturbance of one or more quantities.

words.name: photon
words.description: A photon is an elementary particle that is a quantum of the electromagnetic field, including electromagnetic radiation such as light and radio waves.

words.name: electron
words.description: The electron is a subatomic particle with a negative one elementary electric charge.

words.name: proton
words.description: A proton is a stable subatomic particle, symbol p, with a positive electric charge of +1 e elementary charge.

words.name: neutron
words.description: The neutron is a subatomic particle, symbol n, which has a neutral charge, and a mass slightly greater than that of a proton.

words.name: atom
words.description: Atoms are the basic particles of the chemical elements and the fundamental building blocks of ordinary matter.

words.name: nucleus
words.description: The atomic nucleus is the small, dense region consisting of protons and neutrons at the center of an atom.

words.name: spring
words.description: A spring is an elastic object that stores mechanical energy and is typically used as a mechanical component.

words.name: pendulum
words.description: A pendulum is a weight suspended from a pivot so that it can swing freely.

words.name: lens
words.description: A lens is a transmissive optical device that focuses or disperses a light beam by means of refraction.

words.name: refraction
words.description: In physics, refraction is the redirection of a wave as it passes from one medium to another.

words.name: reflection
words.description: Reflection is the change in direction of a wavefront at an interface between two different media so that the wavefront returns into the medium from which it originated.

words.name: constant
words.description: fixed and well-defined number

words.name: Coulomb
words.description: Charles-Augustin de Coulomb was a French officer, engineer, and physicist; the SI unit of electric charge and Coulomb's law are named after him.

words.name: Ohm
words.description: Georg Simon Ohm was a German physicist and mathematician known for Ohm's law.

words.name: Newton
words.description: Sir Isaac Newton was an English mathematician, physicist, astronomer and author widely recognised as one of the greatest mathematicians and most influential scientists of all time.

words.name: Einstein
words.description: Albert Einstein was a German-born theoretical physicist who is widely held to be one of the greatest and most influential scientists of all time.

words.name: Hooke
words.description: Robert Hooke was an English polymath who was active as a scientist, natural philosopher and architect, and Hooke's law is named after him.

words.name: Bernoulli
words.description: Daniel Bernoulli was a Swiss mathematician and physicist who made important contributions to fluid mechanics.

words.name: Snell
words.description: Willebrord Snellius was a Dutch astronomer and mathematician; the law of refraction is named after him.

words.name: Planck
words.description: Max Planck was a German theoretical physicist whose discovery of energy quanta won him the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1918.

words.name: de Broglie
words.description: Louis de Broglie was a French physicist and aristocrat known for his contributions to quantum theory.

words.name: Stefan
words.description: Josef Stefan was a Slovene physicist, mathematician, and poet of the Austrian Empire; the Stefan-Boltzmann law is partly named after him.

words.name: Wien
words.description: Wilhelm Wien was a German physicist who studied radiation and electromagnetism, winning the 1911 Nobel Prize in Physics.

words.name: Schrödinger
words.description: Erwin Schrödinger was an Austrian-Irish physicist who developed fundamental results in quantum theory.

words.name: Heisenberg
words.description: Werner Heisenberg was a German theoretical physicist and one of the main pioneers of the theory of quantum mechanics.

words.name: Faraday
words.description: Michael Faraday was an English scientist who contributed to the study of electromagnetism and electrochemistry.

words.name: Lorentz
words.description: Hendrik Lorentz was a Dutch physicist who shared the 1902 Nobel Prize in Physics with Pieter Zeeman.

words.name: Ampère
words.description: André-Marie Ampère was a French physicist and mathematician who was one of the founders of the science of classical electromagnetism.

words.name: Gauss
words.description: Carl Friedrich Gauss was a German mathematician and physicist who made significant contributions to many fields.

words.name: G
words.description: G is the gravitational constant, an empirical physical constant involved in the calculation of gravitational effects.

words.name: g
words.description: g is the standard acceleration due to gravity at Earth's surface, approximately 9.80665 m/s².

words.name: c
words.description: c is the speed of light in vacuum, a universal physical constant exactly equal to 299792458 m/s.

words.name: h
words.description: h is the Planck constant, a fundamental physical constant that relates the energy carried by a photon to its frequency.

words.name: ℏ
words.description: ℏ is the reduced Planck constant, equal to the Planck constant divided by 2π.

words.name: kB
words.description: kB is the Boltzmann constant, the proportionality factor that relates the average kinetic energy of particles in a gas with the thermodynamic temperature of the gas.

words.name: R
words.description: R is the molar gas constant, equivalent to the Boltzmann constant multiplied by the Avogadro constant.

words.name: NA
words.description: NA is the Avogadro constant, the proportionality factor that relates the number of constituent particles in a sample with the amount of substance in that sample.

words.name: σ
words.description: σ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant, the constant of proportionality in the Stefan-Boltzmann law.

words.name: ε0
words.description: ε0 is the vacuum permittivity, the absolute permittivity of free space.

words.name: μ0
words.description: μ0 is the vacuum permeability, the magnetic permeability of classical vacuum.

words.name: ke
words.description: ke is the Coulomb constant, the proportionality constant in Coulomb's law.

words.name: first
words.description: the ordinal describing the position one in a sequence, e.g. a first law

words.name: thermodynamics
words.description: the branch of physics that deals with heat, work, temperature and the statistical behaviour of systems.

words.name: physics
words.description: the natural science that studies matter, energy, motion and their interactions; its subareas used here are mechanics, thermodynamics, electromagnetism, optics and quantum mechanics

words.name: mechanics
words.description: the branch of physics that deals with the motion, forces and energy of objects

words.name: electromagnetism
words.description: the branch of physics that deals with electric and magnetic phenomena and their interaction

words.name: optics
words.description: the branch of physics that deals with light and its interaction with matter

words.name: quantum mechanics
words.description: the branch of physics that describes matter and energy at atomic and subatomic scales

triples.name: time unit
triples.description: a unit to measure a duration, used to disambiguate words like 'second' that also have a non-time meaning

triples.name: ranking number
triples.description: an ordinal position in a ranking (first, second, third, ...), used to disambiguate words like 'second' that also are a time unit

triples.name: second (time)
triples.description: the time-unit reading of the ambiguous word 'second'; displayed as 'second' with 'time unit' shown only in the tooltip

triples.name: second (ranking)
triples.description: the ranking-number reading of the ambiguous word 'second'; displayed as 'second' with 'ranking number' shown only in the tooltip

triples.name: first law
triples.description: a law that is the first in a numbered set of laws

triples.name: second law
triples.description: a law that is the second in a numbered set of laws

triples.name: kinetic energy
triples.description: In physics, the kinetic energy of an object is the form of energy that it possesses due to its motion.

triples.name: potential energy
triples.description: In physics, potential energy is the energy held by an object because of its position relative to other objects, stresses within itself, or other factors.

triples.name: internal energy
triples.description: The internal energy of a thermodynamic system is the energy contained within it; it is the energy necessary to create or prepare the system in any given internal state.

triples.name: angular velocity
triples.description: In physics, angular velocity is a pseudovector representation of how the angular position or orientation of an object changes with time.

triples.name: angular momentum
triples.description: In physics, angular momentum is the rotational analog of linear momentum.

triples.name: centripetal acceleration
triples.description: Centripetal acceleration is the acceleration of an object moving in a circular path, directed toward the centre of the circle.

triples.name: moment of inertia
triples.description: The moment of inertia is a quantity expressing a body's tendency to resist angular acceleration; it is the sum of the products of the mass of each particle in the body with the square of its distance from the axis of rotation.

triples.name: Newton's second law
triples.description: Newton's second law of motion states that the net force on an object is equal to the rate of change of its linear momentum with respect to time.

triples.name: Newton's law of gravitation
triples.description: Newton's law of universal gravitation states that every particle attracts every other particle in the universe with a force which is proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between their centres.

triples.name: Hooke's law
triples.description: In physics, Hooke's law is an empirical law which states that the force needed to extend or compress a spring by some distance scales linearly with respect to that distance.

triples.name: Coulomb's law
triples.description: Coulomb's law is an experimental law of physics that calculates the amount of force between two electrically charged particles at rest.

triples.name: Ohm's law
triples.description: Ohm's law states that the current through a conductor between two points is directly proportional to the voltage across the two points.

triples.name: Snell's law
triples.description: Snell's law is a formula used to describe the relationship between the angles of incidence and refraction, when referring to light or other waves passing through a boundary between two different isotropic media.

triples.name: Bernoulli's equation
triples.description: Bernoulli's principle states that an increase in the speed of a fluid occurs simultaneously with a decrease in static pressure or a decrease in the fluid's potential energy.

triples.name: Stefan-Boltzmann law
triples.description: The Stefan-Boltzmann law describes the power radiated from a black body in terms of its temperature.

triples.name: Wien's displacement law
triples.description: Wien's displacement law states that the black-body radiation curve for different temperatures will peak at different wavelengths that are inversely proportional to the temperature.

triples.name: Faraday's law
triples.description: Faraday's law of induction is a basic law of electromagnetism predicting how a magnetic field will interact with an electric circuit to produce an electromotive force.

triples.name: Lorentz force
triples.description: In electromagnetism, the Lorentz force is the force exerted on a charged particle moving with velocity through an electric and magnetic field.

triples.name: Ampère's law
triples.description: Ampère's circuital law relates the integrated magnetic field around a closed loop to the electric current passing through the loop.

triples.name: Gauss's law
triples.description: In physics, Gauss's law relates the distribution of electric charge to the resulting electric field.

triples.name: de Broglie relation
triples.description: The de Broglie hypothesis proposes that all matter exhibits wave-like behaviour, and the de Broglie wavelength of a particle is inversely proportional to its momentum.

triples.name: Planck-Einstein relation
triples.description: The Planck-Einstein relation states that the energy of a photon is proportional to its frequency.

triples.name: Heisenberg uncertainty principle
triples.description: In quantum mechanics, the uncertainty principle is any of a variety of mathematical inequalities asserting a fundamental limit to the accuracy with which the values for certain pairs of physical quantities of a particle can be predicted from initial conditions.

triples.name: Schrödinger equation
triples.description: The Schrödinger equation is a linear partial differential equation that governs the wave function of a quantum-mechanical system.

triples.name: Einstein mass-energy equivalence
triples.description: In physics, mass-energy equivalence is the relationship between mass and energy in a system's rest frame, where the two quantities differ only by a multiplicative constant and the units of measurement.

triples.name: ideal gas law
triples.description: The ideal gas law is the equation of state of a hypothetical ideal gas, relating its pressure, volume, temperature, and amount of substance.

triples.name: first law of thermodynamics
triples.description: The first law of thermodynamics is a formulation of the law of conservation of energy in the context of thermodynamic processes.

triples.name: second law of thermodynamics
triples.description: The second law of thermodynamics is a physical law based on universal empirical observation concerning heat and energy interconversions.

triples.name: specific heat
triples.description: In thermodynamics, the specific heat capacity of a substance is the heat capacity of a sample of the substance divided by the mass of the sample.

triples.name: electric field
triples.description: An electric field is the physical field that surrounds electrically charged particles.

triples.name: magnetic field
triples.description: A magnetic field is a vector field that describes the magnetic influence on moving electric charges, electric currents, and magnetic materials.

triples.name: magnetic flux
triples.description: In physics, specifically electromagnetism, the magnetic flux through a surface is the surface integral of the magnetic field component perpendicular to the surface.

triples.name: electric potential
triples.description: The electric potential at a point in a static electric field is the work needed per unit of charge to move a positive test charge from a reference point to that specific point.

formulas.name: kinematics displacement
formulas.latex: x = x_0 + v_0 t + \tfrac{1}{2} a t^2
